title: Riemann Rarefaction Waves in a Strongly Interacting Fermi Gas
abstract: We investigate the expansion of a homogeneous, strongly interacting Fermi gas released into vacuum in a ``shock tube'' geometry. At unitarity, where the gas is scale invariant and nearly inviscid, we find that the resulting rarefaction wave dynamics are self-similar and in excellent agreement with Riemann's solution of the Euler equation for all temperatures probed. Probing interactions away from unitarity within the BEC-BCS crossover, we observe increasing deviations from the Riemann solution as viscosity increases. However, even on the BCS side, where the sound diffusivity is increased twenty-fold, self-similarity is still approximately preserved. This may reflect how 1D Navier-Stokes rarefaction flows approach Euler self-similar solutions at long times. Our work demonstrates the utility of strongly interacting Fermi gases for the study of nonlinear hydrodynamics in a highly controllable setting.
summary: This work uses strongly interacting Fermi gases to study rarefaction waves in an expanding gas into vacuum. The authors demonstrate that the expansion exhibits strong self-similarity matching Euler theory at unitarity. By analyzing deviations away from this limit, they probe the role of viscosity and thermal effects on nonlinear hydrodynamics in a highly controllable quantum system.
main_problem: The study investigates nonlinear hydrodynamics, specifically rarefaction wave dynamics, when a strongly interacting Fermi gas expands into vacuum.
main_result: Self-similarity in the density profile is robustly observed across various interaction regimes, showing excellent agreement with ideal Euler solutions at unitarity but exhibiting deviations due to viscosity away from it.
method: The authors combine theoretical modeling using 1D hydrodynamics (Euler and Navier-Stokes equations) with experimental measurements of density profiles via polarization rotation imaging.
model_or_system: The system is an ultracold, degenerate Fermi gas (e.g., $^{6}	ext{Li}$) studied across the BEC-BCS crossover, expanding in a 'shock tube' geometry. The dynamics are governed by conservation laws for mass, momentum, and entropy.
key_observables: Normalized density profile ($	ilde{ho}$), scaled coordinates ($	ilde{\xi} = x/t$), sound diffusivity ($D$).
important_parameters: Unitarity limit, BCS side interactions, viscosity ($\eta$), thermal conductivity ($\kappa$), and expansion time ($t$).
main_assumptions: The ideal case assumes isentropic conditions leading to Euler dynamics; deviations require including viscous terms from the full Navier-Stokes equations.
figures_summary: Figures show density profiles collapsing onto self-similar curves when plotted against scaled coordinates, and later figures quantify the deviation from this ideal profile due to viscosity.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the ideal, scale-invariant unitary limit (Euler solution) to testing deviations by incorporating finite viscosity and thermal effects across the BEC-BCS crossover using both theory and experiment.
why_interesting: It provides a highly controllable platform—ultracold atoms—to test fundamental concepts of nonlinear fluid dynamics, allowing direct comparison between ideal continuum theories (Euler) and dissipative reality (Navier-Stokes).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Experimental protocol. (a) A schematic 3D render of our “shock tube” geometry before (left) and after (right) gas release. (b) Typical two-dimensional density profiles for expansion time t = 0 ms (left) and t = 2.5 ms (right).

Figure 2. Self-similar expansion. (a) A collection of normal- ized densities vs. absolute position in the vicinity of x = 0, the original position of the box wall. There are a total of 17 traces, corresponding to 0 ms to 4 ms expansion in steps of 0.25 ms; for clarity, only some traces are labeled. (b) A subset of the data from (a), with expansion times from t = 1.0 ms to t = 4.0 ms, plotted as a function of the reduced coordi- nate ˜ξ ≡ x c0t. The black dashed curve is Eq. 5.
